variables.content:
For a homogeneous three-dimensional electron Fermi liquid with bare electron mass $M$, Landau-quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}$, Fermi momentum $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, and noninteracting density of states at the Fermi level $N_{0}$, and with $F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})$ denoting the $p$-wave ($\ell=1$) component of the Landau interaction amplitude evaluated at equal incoming and outgoing momenta $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, the quasiparticle effective mass satisfies the Landau–Fermi–liquid relation
      $$
      \frac{M^{*}}{M}=\frac{1}{1-\dfrac{N_{0}F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})}{3}}.
      $$
      [8]

For the three-dimensional homogeneous electron liquid of $N$ electrons of bare mass $m$ and charge $-e$ in volume $V$ with particle density $n=N/V$, Fermi wave vector $k_{\mathrm{F}}=(3\pi^2 n)^{1/3}$, Fermi energy $E_{\mathrm{F}}=\hbar^2 k_{\mathrm{F}}^2/(2m)$, and reduced temperature $\theta\equiv k_{\mathrm{B}}T/E_{\mathrm{F}}\ll 1$, Landau Fermi-liquid theory asserts that the low-temperature thermodynamic properties (entropy per particle and heat capacity per particle) are dominated by fermionic quasiparticle excitations associated with a single-particle Green's-function pole characterized by a zero-temperature quasiparticle effective mass $m^*$; equivalently, in this low-$T$ regime the interacting HEL's entropy and heat capacity equal those of a noninteracting Fermi gas of particles with mass $m^*$.
      [18],[19]

Let B denote the coefficient of the T^2 term in the low-temperature expansion w(T)=w_0+B T^2 of the electronic thermal resistivity w(T) for CeCoIn₅. The proposition is that B quantifies inelastic electron-electron scattering in the Fermi-liquid sense and scales proportionally to the square of the quasiparticle effective mass entering transport, B ∝ (m^*)^2, so that an observed strong increase of B(H) as a function of applied magnetic field H corresponds to a strong enhancement (divergence-like increase) of the quasiparticle transport mass m^*(H); here m^* denotes the Landau quasiparticle effective mass that governs transport coefficients in Fermi-liquid theory.

A practical computational scheme for obtaining the temperature- and field-dependent effective mass in a homogeneous heavy-electron liquid is: (i) solve the temperature-dependent Landau integral equation
      1/M*(T,B) = 1/m + ∫[(p_F·p1)/p_F^3] F(p_F,p1;B) [∂n(p1,T,B)/∂p1] d p1/(2π)^3,
      for the self-consistent single-particle spectrum ε(p) and occupation numbers n(p,T,B), where m is the bare electron mass, p_F the Fermi momentum, F(p_F,p1;B) a chosen Landau interaction amplitude possibly dependent on field B, and the momentum integrals are over three-dimensional momentum space; (ii) choose the Landau amplitude coefficients so that ε(p) has an inflection point at p = p_F (i.e., the first and second derivatives dε/dp and d^2ε/dp^2 vanish at p_F), which enforces the fermion-condensation critical condition 1/M* = 0 at T = 0; (iii) compute the thermodynamic entropy S(T,B) per unit volume from the obtained occupations n(p,T,B) using the Fermi-Dirac combinatorial formula S(T,B) = −2 ∫ [n ln n + (1−n) ln(1−n)] d p/(2π)^3 (spin degeneracy 2); and (iv) extract the effective mass operationally via the Landau relation M*(T,B) = S(T,B)/T (with consistent units); this procedure, applied within the homogeneous isotropic heavy-electron liquid model (i.e., neglecting crystal-lattice anisotropy and band structure), yields M*(T,B) exhibiting the interpolating and scaling properties described above.

In a homogeneous, Galilean-invariant electron liquid where the microscopic Hamiltonian conserves total momentum and electrons have bare band mass $m_b$, define the quasiparticle effective mass $m^*$ entering the low-energy dispersion of quasiparticles near the Fermi surface. The proposition is: for the $\ell=1$ density channel Landau parameter $F_1^s$ (defined as $F_1^s=\tfrac{1}{2}(F_1^{\uparrow\uparrow}+F_1^{\uparrow\downarrow})$), the relation $\frac{m_b}{m^*}=\frac{1}{1+F_1^s/d}$ holds (with $d$ the spatial dimensionality), i.e. the usual Fermi-liquid identity connecting band mass, quasiparticle mass, and $F_1^s$ is valid.

The low-energy physics of the two-dimensional electron system at filling fraction $\nu=\tfrac{1}{2}$ is described by a Fermi-liquid-like theory of pseudoparticles (composite objects obtained by binding an electron to two flux quanta), with a well-defined pseudoparticle Fermi momentum $k_F$ and an interaction-generated effective mass $m^*$ such that low-energy collective excitations can be analyzed using Fermi-liquid kinematics (for example, a quadratic dispersion with kinetic energy scale $\sim k_F^2/2m^*$); this statement defines $k_F$ as the wavevector at which the pseudoparticle occupation jumps in the idealized Fermi-liquid picture and defines $m^*$ phenomenologically as the effective quasiparticle mass entering the quadratic dispersion of pseudoparticle excitations.
      Halperin, Lee, and Read, Phys. Rev. B 47, 7312 (1993)

In estimating the thermal-wave speed $v_{\mathrm{s}}$ for conduction electrons in gold, the effective mass is set equal to the free-electron mass ($m^{*}=m$) and the Fermi-liquid interaction parameter is set to zero ($F_{0}^{\mathrm{s}}=0$), so that the general formula
      $$
      v_{\mathrm{s}}=\Bigg(\frac{p_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}}{3\,m\,m^{*}}\big(1+F_{0}^{\mathrm{s}}\big)\Bigg)^{1/2}
      $$
      reduces to $v_{\mathrm{s}}=\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{3}}\,v_{\mathrm{F}}$, where $v_{\mathrm{F}}=p_{\mathrm{F}}/m$ is the Fermi velocity.
      D. Pines and P. Nozi\`eres, The Theory of Quantum Liquids (Benjamin, New York, 1966).

For a metallic state at sufficiently low temperature described by Landau quasiparticles with effective mass m^{*}, the longitudinal electrical resistivity can be represented in the low-T limit by
      ρ_{xx}(T)=ρ_{0}+A\,T^{2},
      where ρ_{0} is the residual (elastic) resistivity and A is a coefficient arising from quasiparticle–quasiparticle electron-electron scattering; in many Fermi-liquid formulations A scales approximately as A ∝ (m^{*})^{2}, so that observation of a regime in which ρ_{xx} is linear in T^{2} at the lowest measured temperatures is taken as evidence for Fermi-liquid transport controlled by electron-electron scattering, and changes in A are interpreted as reflecting changes in the quasiparticle effective mass, provided alternative mechanisms that can mimic T^{2} behavior over limited temperature ranges are absent and the sample is sufficiently homogeneous for conventional Fermi-liquid theory to apply.

Consider electron coupling to marginal-Fermi-liquid fluctuations that renormalize effective mass m^{*}, density of states N^{*}(\theta), and group velocity \mathbf{v}^{*}_{\mathbf{k}}; by analogy with Fermi-liquid transport theory, these renormalizations either cancel exactly in the ratios entering \sigma_{xy} and \kappa_{xy} or else modify only the numerical prefactors of the customary and impurity terms without changing the temperature power laws, so that mass/velocity renormalization does not alter the main conclusion that L_{xy} is a weak function of temperature within a single sample.

The conduction-electron subsystem is modeled as a noninteracting (or weakly interacting) Fermi liquid with single-particle asymptotic energies
      $$
      E_{\\mathbf{k}}^{\\sigma}=\\frac{\\hbar^{2}}{2m}(k_{x}^{2}+k_{y}^{2})-\\sigma J_{K},
      $$
      spin-resolved Fermi wavevectors $k_{F}^{\\sigma}=\\sqrt{k_{F}^{2}+\\sigma K^{2}}$ with $K^{2}\\equiv 2mJ_{K}/\\hbar^{2}$, and spin-resolved densities $n_{\\sigma}$ determined from these Fermi vectors; electron–electron interactions and correlation-induced effective-mass renormalizations are neglected under the assumption that $J_{K}/\\epsilon_{F}\\ll1$, so that the Fermi-liquid picture used in the transport calculations remains valid for the parameter ranges considered.
